audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Samson birth-annunciation and Manoah household narrative, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for unnamed woman's primary revelation, infertility language, prenatal Nazirite instructions, Manoah's repeated inquiry, messenger/name language, wondrous altar scene, the wife's theological reasoning, Samson naming, blessing, and Spirit stirring.

verses.restored: Children of Israel again did evil in eyes of YHWH, and YHWH gave them into hand of Philistines forty years.
There was one man from Zorah, from clan of Danite, and his name was Manoah. His wife had not borne children.
Messenger of YHWH appeared to the woman and said to her, Look, please: you have not borne, but you shall conceive and bear a son.
Now guard yourself, please: do not drink wine or strong drink, and do not eat anything unclean.

For look, you shall conceive and bear a son, and a razor shall not go up over his head, because the boy shall be a Nazirite of Divine from womb; he shall begin to save Israel from hand of Philistines.

The woman came and said to her husband, saying, A man of Divine came to me, and his appearance was like appearance of a messenger of Divine, very awe-inspiring. I did not ask him where he was from, and he did not tell me his name.

He said to me, Look, you shall conceive and bear a son. Now do not drink wine or strong drink, and do not eat any unclean thing, because the boy shall be a Nazirite of Divine from womb until day of his death.
Manoah pleaded with YHWH and said, Please, my Lord, let the man of Divine whom you sent come again to us and teach us what we shall do for the boy who will be born.
Divine listened to voice of Manoah, and messenger of Divine came again to the woman while she was sitting in field; Manoah her husband was not with her.
The woman hurried and ran and told her husband. She said to him, Look, the man who came to me that day has appeared to me.
Manoah rose and went after his wife. He came to the man and said to him, Are you the man who spoke to the woman? He said, I am.
Manoah said, Now let your words come: what shall be rule/way of the boy, and his work?
Messenger of YHWH said to Manoah, From all that I said to the woman, she shall guard herself.
She shall not eat from anything that comes from vine of wine; wine and strong drink she shall not drink, and any unclean thing she shall not eat. All that I commanded her, she shall keep.
Manoah said to messenger of YHWH, Please let us detain you, and let us prepare before you a young goat.

Messenger of YHWH said to Manoah, If you detain me, I will not eat from your bread; and if you prepare an ascending offering, to YHWH you shall bring it up. Manoah did not know that he was messenger of YHWH.
Manoah said to messenger of YHWH, What is your name, so that when your word comes we may honor you?
Messenger of YHWH said to him, Why do you ask this for my name, when it is wondrous/beyond understanding?
Manoah took young goat and grain offering and brought them up on the rock to YHWH; the one was doing wondrously, while Manoah and his wife were watching.
When flame went up from altar toward heavens, messenger of YHWH went up in flame of altar. Manoah and his wife were watching, and they fell upon their faces to ground.
Messenger of YHWH did not again appear to Manoah and his wife. Then Manoah knew that he was messenger of YHWH.
Manoah said to his wife, Dying, we shall die, because we have seen Divine.

His wife said to him, If YHWH desired to put us to death, he would not have accepted from our hand an ascending offering and grain offering, nor shown us all these things, nor at this time made us hear such a thing.
The woman bore a son and called his name Samson. The boy grew, and YHWH blessed him.
Spirit of YHWH began to stir/impel him in Camp of Dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol.

translation_notes: The woman is unnamed, but she is the first recipient of the revelation, receives it twice, accurately transmits it, names the child, and offers the chapter's soundest theological reasoning.
Infertility is a bodily circumstance in the story, not a curse, punishment, shame, lack of femininity, or sign of inferior faith. The woman's value precedes pregnancy.

The dietary and hair instructions belong to this particular Nazirite birth narrative. They may not be generalized into control of pregnant people, reproductive coercion, purity policing, or denial of medical care.
Samson is consecrated before birth without personally choosing the vow. The narrative later exposes the tension between inherited calling, bodily sign, agency, and family expectation.
He will begin to save Israel; the wording anticipates incomplete deliverance rather than a flawless hero.
Manoah asks for instruction, but the messenger repeatedly directs attention back to what was already said to the woman.
Peli in verse 18 is related to wonder and may mean wondrous or beyond understanding; it need not be reduced to merely secret.
Verse 19 has an uncertain subject in 'doing wondrously' and should remain open in later audit.
Manoah fears death after the revelation; his wife reasons from accepted offering, disclosed promise, and Divine intention. Her logic corrects fear without diminishing awe.
YHWH's blessing and Spirit stirring do not certify every later act of Samson as ethical or divinely commanded.
safety_note: Do not shame infertility, childlessness, pregnancy choices, disabled bodies, or people unable or unwilling to reproduce. Do not turn the woman's temporary ritual instructions into universal control over another person's body. Center her revelation, intelligence, authority, and theological clarity; do not erase her because the text does not preserve her name.
